point_001: The proposed framework lacks significant novelty, as it primarily combines well-known existing techniques (gated cross-attention, masked language modeling, contrastive learning, vision-language alignment) rather than introducing fundamentally new mechanisms.
point_002: The use of sentence encoders pre-trained and frozen on NLI and STS datasets is questionable, as these domain-specific features may not generalize well to document understanding domains.
point_003: The baselines used for comparison are not from top ML venues, making it difficult to assess the true significance of the empirical gains; comparisons with stronger, more recent pre-trained language models (e.g., beyond BERT) are missing.
point_004: The model underperforms on the document classification task (RVL-CDIP), and the authors' explanation attributing this to OCR engine differences is insufficiently supported — no quantitative or qualitative analysis comparing OCR engines on that dataset is provided.
point_005: The paper does not evaluate whether the framework works for pure language applications (e.g., GLUE, SQuAD), which undermines the claim of being a 'unified' framework applicable across modalities.
point_006: There is no comparison with word-level pre-training approaches, which may be more appropriate given that some downstream tasks (e.g., entity recognition) are word-oriented rather than sentence-oriented.
point_007: The Vision-Language Alignment pre-training task appears to contribute little performance gain according to the ablation study, raising questions about its necessity.
point_008: The ablation study does not investigate the effect of the gated mechanism in cross-attention — it is unclear whether a standard cross-attention or single-stream Transformer would perform comparably.
point_009: The paper does not explore whether discrete visual representations are necessary for contrastive learning, or whether using RoI features from other in-batch documents as negatives would be a viable alternative.
point_010: The writing quality needs improvement: several key terms (e.g., RoI features, semantic regions) are used before being formally defined, and many method descriptions rely on informal verbal explanations rather than precise mathematical formulations.
point_011: The paper does not investigate whether incorporating standard Masked Language Modeling as an additional pre-training objective would further improve performance.
